standard_description: In the Indian IT sector, non-compete clauses are commonly included in employment contracts but are generally limited in scope. Most reputable IT companies limit non-compete to direct competitors in the same product/service segment, within the city of the employee's last posting, for a period of 6-12 months. Many large IT firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) do not enforce non-compete clauses at all for junior to mid-level employees, recognizing the high attrition rates in the industry and the legal unenforceability under Section 27.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Most IT companies use 6 months for junior roles and 12 months for senior/executive roles.

dimensions.name: Geographic Scope
dimensions.notes: Pan-India non-competes are extremely unusual and always unenforceable.

dimensions.name: Competitor Definition
dimensions.standard_value: Named direct competitors or same product segment
dimensions.notes: The broader the definition, the more aggressive the clause.

source: NASSCOM HR Practices Survey + Industry Expert Analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Notice periods in the Indian IT industry range from 30 days for junior roles to 90 days for senior positions. The industry standard for mid-level engineers is 60 days. Most companies allow notice period buyout (payment in lieu of notice), though some require a minimum serving period. IT services companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL) typically have 90-day notice periods for all levels, while product companies and startups tend to have shorter 30-60 day periods.

dimensions.name: Junior (0-3 years)
dimensions.notes: Some startups allow 15-day notice for junior roles.

dimensions.name: Mid (3-8 years)
dimensions.notes: 60 days is the most common standard in IT product companies.

dimensions.name: Senior (8+ years)
dimensions.notes: 90 days is standard for senior and leadership roles in IT services.

dimensions.name: Notice Buyout
dimensions.notes: Best practice is unconditional buyout option for the employee.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Training bonds in the Indian IT sector are most common in IT services companies that invest in training freshers (typically 2-6 months of structured training). Standard bond periods range from 1-2 years with amounts of ₹50,000-₹2,00,000, roughly proportional to training costs. Product companies and startups rarely use bonds. Bond amounts over ₹3 lakh or periods over 2 years are considered aggressive and are often challenged successfully in courts.

dimensions.name: Bond Duration
dimensions.notes: Bonds over 2 years are rarely upheld by courts.

dimensions.name: Bond Amount
dimensions.notes: Must be proportional to actual training expenditure per court precedents.

dimensions.name: Training Duration
dimensions.notes: Genuine specialized training justifies bonds; standard onboarding does not.

dimensions.name: Pro-rata Reduction
dimensions.notes: Best practice: bond amount decreases monthly during the lock-in period.

source: IT industry HR practices + court precedent analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: In the IT sector, IP assignment clauses typically assign work product created during employment to the employer, limited to work done: (1) during working hours, (2) using company resources, and (3) related to the company's business. Progressive IT companies explicitly carve out personal projects, open-source contributions, and prior inventions. Some companies require employees to declare pre-existing IP through a 'Prior Inventions Schedule.'
typical_range: Work-related IP only, with personal project carve-outs

dimensions.name: Scope
dimensions.standard_value: Work done during employment on company projects
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Only specific deliverables named in contract
dimensions.notes: Good contracts explicitly list what is and isn't assigned.

dimensions.name: Personal Projects
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Strong carve-out with personal IP policy
dimensions.notes: Top tech companies actively encourage side projects with clear ownership.

dimensions.name: Open Source
dimensions.notes: Companies like Google, Microsoft have open-source contribution policies.

dimensions.name: Prior Inventions
dimensions.notes: Best practice: Prior Inventions Schedule attached to contract.

source: IT industry contract analysis + NASSCOM guidelines
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Confidentiality obligations in IT sector contracts typically last 2-3 years after termination, cover specific categories of information (source code, client data, business strategies, trade secrets), and include standard carve-outs for publicly available information, independently developed knowledge, and legally required disclosures. NDAs with indefinite duration or overly broad definitions are considered aggressive.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.lenient_threshold: 1 year or term of contract only
dimensions.notes: Trade secrets may warrant longer protection, but general info should not.

dimensions.name: Scope Definition
dimensions.notes: Best practice: defined categories + marking requirement.

dimensions.name: Standard Carve-outs
dimensions.standard_value: Public info, prior knowledge, independent development, legal obligations
dimensions.notes: All 4 standard carve-outs should be present.

dimensions.name: Portfolio Use
dimensions.standard_value: Can mention client name and general role
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Full portfolio use with client approval
dimensions.notes: Important for freelancers and consultants building their portfolio.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Standard IT sector employment contracts allow termination by either party with the agreed notice period. Termination for cause (misconduct, performance failure) typically requires a documented process: verbal warning → written warning → performance improvement plan → termination. Immediate termination without notice is reserved for serious misconduct (fraud, data theft, criminal activity). Fair contracts include termination symmetry — both parties have equal termination rights.
typical_range: Mutual termination rights with notice period

dimensions.name: Symmetry
dimensions.standard_value: Both parties can terminate with equal notice
dimensions.strict_threshold: Company can terminate immediately; employee has long notice
dimensions.notes: Fair contracts have equal termination provisions.

dimensions.name: For-Cause Process
dimensions.notes: Performance-based termination should follow a documented process.

dimensions.name: Severance
dimensions.notes: Large IT companies often offer voluntary separation packages.

dimensions.name: Final Settlement Timeline
dimensions.notes: Best practice: full and final settlement within 30 days of last working day.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: In IT consulting and service contracts, liability is typically capped at the total contract value or the fees paid in the last 12 months. Both parties mutually exclude consequential, indirect, and special damages. Professional indemnity insurance is common for IT service providers, with coverage of ₹10-50 lakh for consulting firms and higher for large IT companies. Uncapped liability is extremely unusual and considered aggressive.
typical_range: Capped at 1x contract value, mutual consequential damage exclusion

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Capped at fees in last 3 months
dimensions.notes: Some contracts cap at 12-month fees for ongoing engagements.

dimensions.name: Consequential Damages
dimensions.notes: Mutual exclusion of consequential damages is industry standard.

dimensions.name: Indemnification
dimensions.notes: Indemnification should be mutual and proportional.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Standard payment terms in IT services and freelance contracts range from Net-15 to Net-30. Milestone-based payments typically follow a structured schedule: 20-30% advance, milestones at key deliverables, and 10-20% on final acceptance. Late payment interest of 1.5-2% per month is common. Net-45 or Net-60 is sometimes seen in large enterprise contracts but is above industry standard for individual freelancers.
typical_range: Net-15 to Net-30, or milestone-based with advance

dimensions.name: Payment Cycle
dimensions.notes: Net-30 is the most common standard for B2B IT services.

dimensions.name: Advance Payment
dimensions.notes: Freelancers should always insist on an advance payment.

dimensions.name: Late Payment Interest
dimensions.notes: Late payment interest should be explicitly stated.

dimensions.name: Acceptance Period
dimensions.notes: Deemed acceptance (silence = acceptance) protects the service provider.

source: IT freelance market analysis + platform standards
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: IT sector contracts typically include a tiered dispute resolution mechanism: (1) good-faith negotiation between designated representatives, (2) mediation if negotiation fails (within 30 days), and (3) arbitration as a final step (under Indian Arbitration Act, 1996). Courts are typically the last resort. Arbitration venue is usually the city of the employee's workplace or the company's registered office. Mutually appointed arbitrators are standard.

dimensions.name: First Step
dimensions.notes: Negotiation should always be the first step to avoid costs.

dimensions.name: Arbitrator Selection
dimensions.notes: Unilateral appointment is invalid per Perkins Eastman (2019 SC).

dimensions.name: Venue
dimensions.strict_threshold: Company's HQ in a distant city
dimensions.notes: Venue should be convenient for the weaker party.

dimensions.name: Cost Sharing
dimensions.standard_value: Shared equally or by losing party
dimensions.notes: Cost-sharing prevents frivolous claims from both sides.

source: IT industry standard dispute resolution practices
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Consulting firms typically have non-compete clauses that restrict consultants from working with direct competitors or soliciting clients they directly served. Non-compete durations are typically 6-12 months for associates and up to 12-18 months for partners/senior consultants. Non-solicitation of clients is often more strictly enforced than general non-compete, especially for client-facing roles.
typical_range: 6-18 months depending on seniority, limited to direct clients

dimensions.name: Duration (Associate)
dimensions.notes: Junior consultants rarely face enforced non-competes.

dimensions.name: Duration (Partner/Senior)
dimensions.notes: Senior roles with client relationships face longer restrictions.

dimensions.name: Client Non-Solicitation
dimensions.standard_value: Clients directly served in last 12 months
dimensions.notes: Non-solicitation of direct clients is more enforceable than general non-compete.

dimensions.name: Geographic Scope
dimensions.notes: Consulting non-competes are usually limited to the practice area.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Independent consultants in India typically work on monthly retainer or project-based payment models. Retainers are billed monthly with Net-15 to Net-30 terms. Project-based work follows milestone billing with 25-50% advance, interim milestones, and 10-25% on completion. Large consulting firms billing corporate clients may have Net-30 to Net-45 terms, but individual consultants should insist on Net-15 to Net-30.
typical_range: Net-15 to Net-30, 25-50% advance for project work

dimensions.name: Retainer Payment
dimensions.notes: Retainers should be paid at the start or within 15 days of the billing period.

dimensions.name: Project Advance
dimensions.notes: Higher advances for new client relationships.

dimensions.name: Milestone Clarity
dimensions.notes: Clear milestones prevent payment delays.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Across industries in India, post-employment non-compete clauses are generally unenforceable under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act. The standard practice — regardless of industry — is that employers include non-compete clauses as a deterrent but rarely enforce them. Enforceable alternatives include non-solicitation of clients and non-disclosure of trade secrets, with typical durations of 6-12 months.
typical_range: Generally unenforceable; non-solicitation of 6-12 months is the enforceable alternative

dimensions.name: Enforceability
dimensions.strict_threshold: N/A — all post-employment non-competes are void
dimensions.notes: Section 27, Indian Contract Act, 1872 renders these void.

dimensions.name: Non-Solicitation Alternative
dimensions.notes: Non-solicitation may be enforceable if reasonable.

dimensions.name: Non-Disclosure Alternative
dimensions.notes: NDA is the most enforceable alternative to non-compete.

source: Legal analysis of Section 27, ICA 1872 + cross-industry survey
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Across industries in India, notice periods typically range from 15-90 days depending on the role level and industry. State-specific Shops & Establishments Acts often set minimum notice requirements (commonly 15-30 days). The general principle is symmetry — notice periods should be equal for both employer and employee. Payment in lieu of notice (notice buyout) is standard practice.
typical_range: 15-90 days with symmetry and buyout option

dimensions.name: Entry Level
dimensions.notes: State laws often cap at 30 days for entry-level roles.

dimensions.name: Mid Level
dimensions.notes: 30 days is the most common cross-industry standard.

dimensions.name: Senior Level
dimensions.notes: Senior leadership roles may have 90-day notice.

dimensions.name: Buyout Option
dimensions.notes: Employees should always have the option to buy out notice.

source: Cross-industry employment survey + state labor law analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Employment bonds across Indian industries are only justified when the employer provides genuine, specialized training. Standard bond periods are 1-2 years with amounts proportional to actual training costs. Courts consistently apply the proportionality test — the bond amount must reflect actual expenditure, not an arbitrary penalty. Standard onboarding or induction does NOT justify a bond.
typical_range: 1-2 years, proportional to actual training cost

dimensions.name: Justification
dimensions.notes: Courts look for genuine investment in the employee's skills.

dimensions.name: Amount
dimensions.notes: Courts reduce disproportionate amounts to actual costs.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Lock-in should be proportional to training duration.

dimensions.name: Pro-rata Clause
dimensions.strict_threshold: Full penalty regardless of time served
dimensions.notes: Pro-rata reduction is fair and increasingly expected by courts.

source: Cross-industry legal analysis + court precedent review
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: The cross-industry standard for liability in service contracts is a cap equal to the total contract value or fees paid in the preceding 12 months. Both parties mutually exclude consequential, indirect, and special damages. Indemnification should be mutual and limited to the indemnifying party's negligence or breach. Uncapped liability and one-sided indemnification are considered aggressive and non-standard.
typical_range: Capped at 1x contract value, mutual damage exclusions

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.notes: Some industries (finance, healthcare) may have higher caps.

dimensions.name: Consequential Damages
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Fully excluded for both with carve-outs for IP infringement
dimensions.notes: IP infringement and data breach may be carved out from exclusion.

dimensions.name: Indemnification Direction
dimensions.notes: Mutual indemnification is the professional standard.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Standard confidentiality obligations across industries in India last 2-5 years after termination. Confidential information is defined in specific categories (trade secrets, client data, business plans, financial information). All standard NDAs include four carve-outs: (1) publicly available information, (2) information already known, (3) independently developed information, and (4) information received from a third party without restriction.
typical_range: 2-5 years post-termination, defined categories, standard carve-outs

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Trade secrets may warrant perpetual protection; general business info should not.

dimensions.name: Scope
dimensions.notes: Category-based definitions are clearer and more enforceable.

dimensions.name: Carve-outs
dimensions.notes: Missing carve-outs make the NDA unreasonably broad.

dimensions.name: Return/Destruction
dimensions.strict_threshold: Retention rights for the disclosing party only
dimensions.notes: Best practice: destroy/return with certification within 30 days.

source: Cross-industry NDA analysis + legal practice standards
risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Standard employment compensation in India has a fixed-to-variable ratio of 70:30 to 85:15 for most roles. Variable pay criteria should be clearly defined with measurable KPIs. CTC (Cost to Company) typically includes basic salary, HRA, special allowance, PF employer contribution, and gratuity. Discretionary bonuses without criteria are increasingly rare in structured organizations. Variable pay should have documented criteria shared at the start of the performance period.

dimensions.name: Fixed vs Variable
dimensions.notes: Sales roles may have 50:50 but should have clear commission structures.

dimensions.name: Variable Pay Criteria
dimensions.notes: Variable pay criteria must be transparent and measurable.

dimensions.name: Payment Frequency
dimensions.standard_value: Monthly salary by 7th of following month
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Salary on last working day of month
dimensions.notes: Payment of Wages Act mandates payment within 7-10 days.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: Standard dispute resolution across industries follows a tiered approach: negotiation first (15-30 days), then mediation (30 days), then arbitration or litigation. For employment disputes, labour courts have jurisdiction for workmen. For consumer disputes, consumer commissions cannot be ousted by contract. Arbitration is increasingly preferred over litigation for its speed and confidentiality.

dimensions.name: Escalation Process
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Optional mediation with court as final recourse
dimensions.notes: Tiered approach saves time and money for both parties.

dimensions.name: Venue
dimensions.standard_value: Place of performance or mutual agreement
dimensions.strict_threshold: Exclusive jurisdiction of company's HQ city
dimensions.notes: Non-exclusive jurisdiction is fairer for individuals.

dimensions.name: Governing Law
dimensions.strict_threshold: Foreign governing law for domestic contracts
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Laws of India with state specification
dimensions.notes: Domestic contracts should always be governed by Indian law.

risk_if_exceeded: Deviating from this standard introduces asymmetric risk and potential exploitation.
recommended_clause_language: Language should strictly reflect the standard practice without hidden contingencies.
red_flag_language: Watch for absolute terms ("perpetual", "uncapped", "sole discretion") that bypass the standard.

standard_description: A clause preventing employees from joining competitors for a certain period post-employment.
risk_if_exceeded: May be deemed unenforceable by courts, leading to potential legal disputes.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees not to engage in any business activities that compete with the company for a period of 6 months post-employment.
red_flag_language: The employee is prohibited from working in the industry for 24 months post-employment.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should be reasonable and justifiable.

standard_description: The period an employee must serve after resigning before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Extended notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and potential legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The employee shall provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The employee must serve a notice period of 6 months.

dimensions.name: Notice Duration
dimensions.notes: Should align with industry standards and employee role.

standard_description: Intellectual Property clause defining ownership of work created during employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Ambiguity can lead to disputes over ownership of creative work.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created during employment shall be the sole property of the company.
red_flag_language: Employee retains rights to all work created during employment.

dimensions.name: Ownership
dimensions.notes: Clear definitions prevent future disputes.

standard_description: Defines the extent of liability for parties in case of breach or negligence.
risk_if_exceeded: Unlimited liability can lead to significant financial exposure.
recommended_clause_language: The company's liability shall be limited to direct damages not exceeding the contract value.
red_flag_language: The company shall be liable for all damages, including indirect and consequential.

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.notes: Caps should be reasonable and industry-aligned.

standard_description: Obligation to keep company information confidential during and after employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Lack of confidentiality can result in loss of competitive advantage.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees to maintain confidentiality of all proprietary information indefinitely.
red_flag_language: Confidentiality obligations end upon termination of employment.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should ensure protection of sensitive information.

standard_description: A clause restricting employees from engaging in competing activities post-employment.
risk_if_exceeded: May be deemed unenforceable if overly restrictive.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees not to engage in any business that competes with the company for a period of 6 months following termination.
red_flag_language: The employee is prohibited from working in any capacity in the design industry for 2 years.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should be reasonable and justifiable.

standard_description: The period an employee must serve after resigning before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Extended notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and potential legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The employee shall provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The employee must provide a notice period of 6 months.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Should balance company needs and employee flexibility.

standard_description: Clauses related to the ownership of intellectual property created during employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Ambiguity can lead to disputes over IP ownership.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created by the employee during the term of employment shall be owned by the employer.
red_flag_language: The employee retains all rights to intellectual property created during employment.

dimensions.name: Ownership
dimensions.notes: Clear assignment is crucial to avoid disputes.

standard_description: Clauses that limit the liability of the company in case of disputes or damages.
risk_if_exceeded: Excessive limitation can be challenged as unfair.
recommended_clause_language: The company's liability shall be limited to direct damages not exceeding the total fees paid.
red_flag_language: The company shall have no liability for any damages whatsoever.

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.notes: Should be reasonable and justifiable.

standard_description: A clause preventing employees from joining competitors for a specified period after leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: May be deemed unenforceable if too restrictive, leading to potential legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees not to engage in any business that competes with the company for a period of 6 months post-termination.
red_flag_language: The employee is prohibited from working in any capacity in the industry for 24 months post-termination.

dimensions.name: duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should be reasonable and justifiable based on role.

standard_description: The period an employee must serve after resigning before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Extended notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and potential legal disputes.
recommended_clause_language: The employee is required to serve a notice period of 2 months upon resignation.
red_flag_language: The employee must serve a notice period of 6 months upon resignation.

dimensions.name: duration
dimensions.notes: Should align with industry standards and role requirements.

standard_description: Intellectual Property clause ensuring company ownership of work-related creations.
risk_if_exceeded: Failure to secure IP rights can lead to loss of proprietary information and competitive advantage.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created during employment shall be owned by the company.
red_flag_language: The employee retains rights to all intellectual property created during employment.

dimensions.name: ownership
dimensions.notes: Clear assignment of rights is crucial to avoid disputes.

standard_description: Defines the extent of liability an employee holds for actions taken during employment.
typical_range: Limited to gross negligence or willful misconduct
risk_if_exceeded: Overly broad liability can deter talent and lead to legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The employee shall be liable only for losses arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct.
red_flag_language: The employee is liable for any and all losses incurred by the company.

dimensions.name: scope
dimensions.notes: Liability should be fair and not overly burdensome.

standard_description: A clause restricting employees from joining competitors for a specified period post-employment.
risk_if_exceeded: May be deemed unenforceable due to excessive restriction on employee mobility.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee agrees not to engage in any business similar to the Company’s business for a period of 6 months within a 50 km radius.
red_flag_language: The Employee agrees not to work in any capacity in the industry for 24 months post-termination.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should be reasonable and justifiable.

dimensions.name: Geographic Scope
dimensions.notes: Scope should be limited to areas where the company operates.

standard_description: A clause specifying the duration of notice required for termination of employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Long notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and potential legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee shall provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The Employee must provide a notice period of 6 months.

dimensions.name: Notice Duration
dimensions.notes: Should balance company needs and employee flexibility.

standard_description: A clause addressing the ownership of intellectual property created during employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Ambiguity can lead to disputes over IP ownership.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created by the Employee during the term of employment shall be the sole property of the Company.
red_flag_language: The Employee retains rights to all intellectual property created during employment.

dimensions.name: Ownership
dimensions.notes: Clear assignment to avoid future disputes.

standard_description: A clause limiting the liability of the employer for certain actions or omissions.
risk_if_exceeded: Excessive limitation may be unenforceable and lead to reputational damage.
recommended_clause_language: The Company’s liability shall be limited to direct damages up to the amount of the Employee’s annual salary.
red_flag_language: The Company shall not be liable for any damages, direct or indirect.

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.notes: Should be reasonable and proportionate to potential risks.

standard_description: Non-compete clauses restrict employees from joining competitors for a specified period after leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Potential legal challenges and difficulty in enforcement.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees not to engage in any business or employment with a competitor for a period of 6 months post-termination.
red_flag_language: The employee is prohibited from working in any capacity in the industry for 24 months.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Longer durations may be unenforceable.

standard_description: Notice period clauses define the time frame an employee must provide before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Operational disruptions and potential legal disputes.
recommended_clause_language: The employee shall provide a notice period of 60 days prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The employee must provide a notice period of 180 days.

dimensions.name: Notice Duration
dimensions.notes: Excessive notice periods may be challenged.

standard_description: Intellectual Property clauses ensure that all work created by employees is owned by the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Loss of proprietary information and competitive advantage.
recommended_clause_language: The employee assigns all rights, title, and interest in any work created during employment to the company.
red_flag_language: The employee retains rights to any work created during employment.

dimensions.name: IP Ownership
dimensions.notes: Ensure clear assignment to avoid disputes.

standard_description: Liability clauses limit the company's liability for damages arising from the employee's actions.
risk_if_exceeded: Increased financial exposure and potential litigation.
recommended_clause_language: The company's liability is limited to direct damages and excludes consequential damages.
red_flag_language: The company is liable for all damages, including indirect and consequential damages.

dimensions.name: Liability Limitation
dimensions.notes: Clearly define the scope of liability.

standard_description: Confidentiality clauses protect sensitive company information from being disclosed by employees.
risk_if_exceeded: Loss of sensitive information and competitive disadvantage.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all proprietary information indefinitely.
red_flag_language: The confidentiality obligation ends upon termination of employment.

dimensions.name: Confidentiality Duration
dimensions.notes: Indefinite duration is standard to ensure ongoing protection.

standard_description: Non-compete clauses in early-stage startups are designed to prevent employees from joining competitors or starting a competing business for a certain period after leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Exceeding 12 months may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, leading to potential legal disputes.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee agrees not to engage in any business activities that directly compete with the Company for a period of 12 months following the termination of employment.
red_flag_language: The Employee shall not engage in any business activities in any capacity for a period of 24 months following termination.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should balance enforceability with business protection.

standard_description: Notice periods in early-stage startups are typically short to allow flexibility and quick adaptation to changing business needs.
risk_if_exceeded: Long notice periods can hinder agility and may lead to increased costs if the employee is not productive during the notice period.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee shall provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The Employee shall provide a notice period of 6 months prior to resignation.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Shorter notice periods are preferred for flexibility.

standard_description: Intellectual Property (IP) clauses ensure that any IP created by employees during their tenure belongs to the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Failure to secure IP rights can lead to disputes over ownership and potential loss of competitive advantage.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee agrees to assign all rights, title, and interest in any intellectual property created during employment to the Company.
red_flag_language: The Employee retains rights to any intellectual property created during employment.

dimensions.name: Scope
dimensions.notes: Ensure comprehensive coverage of IP rights.

standard_description: Liability clauses in early-stage startups limit the company's liability for damages arising from the use of its products or services.
risk_if_exceeded: Broad liability can expose the company to significant financial risk and potential bankruptcy.
recommended_clause_language: The Company's liability is limited to direct damages and shall not exceed the amount paid by the customer in the last 12 months.
red_flag_language: The Company shall be liable for all damages, including indirect and consequential damages.

dimensions.name: Scope of Liability
dimensions.notes: Limit liability to maintain financial stability.

standard_description: Non-compete clauses prevent employees from joining competitors for a specified period after leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: May be deemed unenforceable due to being overly restrictive.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees not to engage in any business that competes with the company for a period of 6 months post-termination.
red_flag_language: The employee is prohibited from working in the consulting industry for 2 years post-termination.

dimensions.name: Duration
dimensions.notes: Shorter durations are more enforceable.

standard_description: Notice period clauses define the time an employee must provide before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Long notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and potential legal challenges.
recommended_clause_language: The employee shall provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The employee must provide a notice period of 6 months.

dimensions.name: Notice Duration
dimensions.notes: Balance between operational needs and employee flexibility.

standard_description: Intellectual Property clauses ensure that any IP created by employees during their tenure belongs to the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Lack of clarity can lead to disputes over ownership.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created during the term of employment shall be the sole property of the company.
red_flag_language: The employee retains rights to all intellectual property created.

dimensions.name: IP Assignment
dimensions.notes: Clear assignment prevents future disputes.

standard_description: Liability clauses limit the company's responsibility for damages arising from the employee's actions.
risk_if_exceeded: Unlimited liability can expose the company to significant financial risk.
recommended_clause_language: The company's liability is limited to direct damages and shall not exceed the total fees paid under this agreement.
red_flag_language: The company is liable for all damages, including indirect and consequential damages.

dimensions.name: Liability Cap
dimensions.notes: Limiting liability protects the company's financial interests.

standard_description: Confidentiality clauses protect sensitive company information from being disclosed by employees.
risk_if_exceeded: Overly broad clauses may be unenforceable.
recommended_clause_language: The employee agrees to keep all company information confidential indefinitely.
red_flag_language: The employee may disclose company information after 1 year.

dimensions.name: Confidentiality Duration
dimensions.notes: Indefinite duration ensures long-term protection.

standard_description: Non-compete clauses prevent employees from joining competitors or starting a competing business for a specified period after leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Exceeding 12 months may be considered unreasonable and unenforceable in court, leading to potential legal disputes.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee agrees not to engage in any business activities that compete with the Company for a period of 12 months following the termination of employment.
red_flag_language: The Employee shall not work in any capacity in the retail industry for 24 months after leaving the Company.

dimensions.name: duration
dimensions.notes: Duration should be reasonable to ensure enforceability.

standard_description: Notice period clauses specify the amount of time an employee must provide notice before leaving the company.
risk_if_exceeded: Long notice periods can lead to employee dissatisfaction and may be challenged in court.
recommended_clause_language: The Employee is required to provide a notice period of 2 months prior to resignation.
red_flag_language: The Employee must provide a notice period of 6 months before leaving the Company.

dimensions.name: notice_duration
dimensions.notes: Ensure notice period aligns with industry standards to avoid disputes.

standard_description: Intellectual Property clauses define the ownership and usage rights of creations and inventions developed during employment.
risk_if_exceeded: Failure to clearly define IP rights can lead to disputes over ownership and usage.
recommended_clause_language: All intellectual property created by the Employee during the term of employment shall be the sole property of the Company.
red_flag_language: The Employee retains rights to all intellectual property created during employment.

dimensions.name: ownership
dimensions.notes: Ensure clarity in IP ownership to prevent future disputes.

standard_description: Liability clauses limit the extent of responsibility one party has in case of damages or losses.
risk_if_exceeded: Broad liability can expose the company to significant financial risk.
recommended_clause_language: The Company's liability is limited to direct damages and shall not exceed the total fees paid under this agreement.
red_flag_language: The Company is liable for all types of damages, including indirect and consequential damages.

dimensions.name: liability_limit
dimensions.notes: Limiting liability to direct damages is standard practice.

standard_description: Standard e-commerce refund policies in India provide 7-15 day return windows for most products, with full refund to the original payment method within 5-7 business days of return receipt. Electronics typically have shorter windows (7 days) while fashion has longer (15-30 days). Perishable and personalized items are commonly excluded. The Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020 require clear disclosure of return/refund policies before purchase.
typical_range: 7-15 day return window, refund within 5-7 business days

dimensions.name: Return Window
dimensions.notes: Fashion/apparel typically has longer windows than electronics.

dimensions.name: Refund Method
dimensions.notes: Consumer has the right to refund in original payment mode.

dimensions.name: Refund Timeline
dimensions.notes: Delays beyond 15 days are considered deficiency in service.

dimensions.name: Return Shipping
dimensions.notes: Defective/wrong item returns should always be seller-paid.

risk_if_exceeded: No-refund or store-credit-only policies violate consumer protection rights and can be challenged at Consumer Forum.
recommended_clause_language: Returns accepted within 15 days of delivery. Full refund to original payment method within 7 business days of return receipt.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'all sales are final', 'exchange only', 'store credit only', or return windows shorter than 3 days.

standard_description: E-commerce platforms in India have obligations under the Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020 to verify seller identity, ensure product authenticity, and provide grievance redressal. While platforms operating as 'marketplaces' have reduced direct product liability compared to 'inventory' models, they must still facilitate returns, provide buyer protection, and appoint a Grievance Officer accessible within 48 hours.
typical_range: Shared liability between platform and seller, with buyer protection programs

dimensions.name: Seller Verification
dimensions.standard_value: Platform verifies seller identity and GST
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Full background checks and product testing
dimensions.notes: E-Commerce Rules mandate seller identity verification.

dimensions.name: Buyer Protection
dimensions.notes: Major platforms offer buyer protection programs.

dimensions.name: Grievance Redressal
dimensions.notes: E-Commerce Rules mandate Grievance Officer appointment.

dimensions.name: Counterfeit Protection
dimensions.notes: Platform should have counterfeit reporting mechanism.

risk_if_exceeded: Platforms that disclaim all seller liability may face Consumer Forum complaints and CCPA action.
recommended_clause_language: Platform shall facilitate returns and refunds for defective products and provide a Grievance Officer accessible within 48 hours.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'we are merely a marketplace', 'no liability for seller products', or absence of buyer protection program.

standard_description: E-commerce platforms collect extensive personal data (purchase history, browsing behavior, payment info, addresses). Standard practice requires explicit consent for data collection, clear disclosure of data usage, data minimization, and secure storage compliant with IT Act Section 43A and DPDP Act 2023. Sharing personal data with third-party advertisers should require separate consent.
typical_range: Consent-based collection, purpose-limited use, 'reasonable security' standards

dimensions.name: Consent Mechanism
dimensions.notes: DPDP Act requires 'clear and affirmative' consent.

dimensions.name: Data Sharing
dimensions.standard_value: Disclosed third parties only, with consent
dimensions.notes: Third-party data sharing requires separate explicit consent.

dimensions.name: Data Deletion
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Instant account deletion with data purge
dimensions.notes: DPDP Act grants right to erasure.

dimensions.name: Breach Notification
dimensions.notes: DPDP Act mandates breach notification to DPB.

risk_if_exceeded: Non-compliance with DPDP Act can result in penalties up to ₹250 crore.
recommended_clause_language: Data collected with explicit opt-in consent. Used only for stated purposes. Deletion available within 30 days of request.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'by using the service you consent to all data collection', blanket data sharing with 'affiliates and partners', or no deletion option.

standard_description: E-commerce dispute resolution in India follows a tiered system: (1) platform internal resolution (Grievance Officer), (2) National Consumer Helpline (1800-11-4000), (3) District/State/National Consumer Commission. Platforms cannot contractually prevent consumers from approaching Consumer Forums. Online filing via edaakhil.nic.in is available. Most platforms have internal resolution timelines of 7-15 days.
typical_range: Internal resolution (7-15 days) → Consumer Forum (statutory right)

dimensions.name: Internal Resolution Timeline
dimensions.notes: Most major platforms resolve within 7 days.

dimensions.name: Escalation Path
dimensions.notes: Consumer Forum access is a statutory right that cannot be waived.

dimensions.name: Burden of Proof
dimensions.notes: Delivery proof is the seller/platform's responsibility.

risk_if_exceeded: Arbitration clauses that attempt to oust Consumer Forum jurisdiction are void and unenforceable.
recommended_clause_language: Disputes shall first be raised with the Grievance Officer. Consumer's right to approach Consumer Forum remains unaffected.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'mandatory binding arbitration', 'exclusive jurisdiction of courts in [distant city]', or 'waiver of Consumer Forum rights'.

standard_description: Standard e-commerce delivery in India ranges from 2-7 business days for domestic orders in metro cities, extending to 7-14 days for tier-2/3 cities and remote areas. Express delivery (next-day or same-day) is available in major metros. Delivery date commitments should be clearly stated at checkout. Delayed delivery beyond the promised date entitles the buyer to cancel and receive a full refund.

dimensions.name: Metro Delivery
dimensions.notes: Major metros have 2-3 day standard delivery.

dimensions.name: Delivery Tracking
dimensions.notes: Tracking is expected for all online purchases.

dimensions.name: Delayed Delivery Remedy
dimensions.standard_value: Right to cancel with full refund
dimensions.notes: Consumer can cancel if delivery exceeds promised date.

dimensions.name: Delivery Charges
dimensions.notes: Delivery charges must be disclosed before payment.

risk_if_exceeded: Repeated delivery failures or hidden charges can be reported to Consumer Forum and CCPA.
recommended_clause_language: Estimated delivery within 5-7 business days. Full refund available if delivery exceeds promised date by more than 3 days.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'delivery dates are estimates only', 'no refund for delayed delivery', or hidden charges added at final checkout.

standard_description: Standard personal and home loan terms in India include fixed or floating interest rates linked to an external benchmark (repo rate), with a clearly stated spread. Loan tenure ranges from 1-30 years depending on loan type. RBI mandates transparency in total cost disclosure (processing fee, GST, insurance), and all floating-rate loans since October 2019 must be linked to an external benchmark (EBLR/repo rate).

dimensions.name: Interest Rate Type
dimensions.notes: All new floating loans must be EBLR-linked (RBI mandate).

dimensions.name: Processing Fee
dimensions.notes: Must be disclosed upfront before disbursement.

dimensions.name: Total Cost Disclosure
dimensions.notes: Flat rate advertising is misleading — always check reducing balance rate.

dimensions.name: Loan Agreement Transparency
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Draft agreement for review + cooling-off period
dimensions.notes: Borrower must receive all terms before commitment.

source: RBI Fair Practices Code + lending market analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Non-disclosure of terms or misleading rate advertising violates RBI Fair Practices Code and can be reported to Banking Ombudsman.
recommended_clause_language: Interest rate: [Repo Rate + X% spread]. All fees disclosed upfront. Full amortization schedule provided before disbursement.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'flat rate' advertising (misleading), undisclosed processing fees, or internal benchmarks (BPLR) instead of external benchmarks.

standard_description: RBI has eliminated prepayment/foreclosure charges on all floating-rate home loans. For fixed-rate home loans and personal loans, prepayment charges are typically 2-4% of outstanding principal, reducing over the loan tenure. Part-prepayment is generally allowed annually (10-25% of outstanding) without charges. NBFCs may have different terms but must comply with RBI's Fair Practices Code.
typical_range: Zero for floating home loans; 2-4% for fixed-rate/personal loans

dimensions.name: Floating Home Loan
dimensions.strict_threshold: Any prepayment charge on floating home loan
dimensions.notes: RBI banned prepayment charges on floating home loans in 2012.

dimensions.name: Fixed Home Loan
dimensions.notes: Should reduce as loan ages — e.g., 3% in year 1, 1% in year 5.

dimensions.name: Part-Prepayment Allowance
dimensions.notes: Part-prepayment helps reduce interest burden significantly.

dimensions.name: Personal Loan
dimensions.standard_value: 2-4% of outstanding, with lock-in of 6-12 months
dimensions.notes: Personal loan prepayment charges are higher but should still be transparent.

source: RBI Circular DBOD.Dir.BC.No.8/13.03.00/2012-13 + lending practices analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Charging prepayment penalties on floating home loans violates RBI directives. Report to Banking Ombudsman.
recommended_clause_language: No prepayment/foreclosure charges on floating-rate home loans. Part-prepayment of up to 25% annually without charges.
red_flag_language: Watch for prepayment charges on floating home loans (illegal), undisclosed lock-in periods, or charges that don't reduce over time.

standard_description: Personal guarantors for business loans face significant liability under Indian law. The IBC (2016, amended 2021) now allows insolvency proceedings against personal guarantors. Standard practice is for guarantees to be limited in amount, require the lender to exhaust remedies against the borrower first, and release the guarantor when they cease to be associated with the borrowing entity.

dimensions.name: Guarantee Amount
dimensions.standard_value: Capped at loan amount or specific sum
dimensions.notes: Unlimited guarantees are extremely aggressive.

dimensions.name: Exhaustion Requirement
dimensions.strict_threshold: Co-extensive liability (lender can pursue guarantor directly)
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Lender must exhaust ALL borrower assets first
dimensions.notes: Indian Contract Act Sections 128-145 govern this.

dimensions.name: Release Mechanism
dimensions.notes: Guarantor should be released when they lose control/association.

dimensions.name: IBC Exposure
dimensions.standard_value: Subject to IBC personal guarantor provisions
dimensions.notes: IBC 2016 (Section 95) allows proceedings against personal guarantors.

risk_if_exceeded: Unlimited personal guarantees combined with IBC provisions can result in insolvency proceedings against the guarantor personally.
recommended_clause_language: Guarantee limited to [amount]. Lender shall exhaust remedies against Borrower before invoking guarantee. Guarantee releases upon Guarantor ceasing directorship.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'unconditional and irrevocable guarantee', 'co-extensive liability', 'guarantee survives cessation of directorship', or 'including all future liabilities'.

standard_description: Since October 2019, RBI mandates all new floating-rate retail loans be linked to an external benchmark — primarily the repo rate (currently ~6.5%). The spread over the benchmark is fixed for the loan tenure. Interest rate changes must be transmitted within 3 months of a repo rate change. For fixed-rate loans, the rate remains constant for the agreed period.
typical_range: Repo rate + 2-4% spread for home loans; higher spread for personal/business loans

dimensions.name: Benchmark
dimensions.notes: All new floating loans must be EBLR-linked since Oct 2019.

dimensions.name: Spread
dimensions.notes: The spread should not change once fixed at disbursement.

dimensions.name: Rate Transmission
dimensions.notes: Banks have been criticized for slow rate cuts but fast rate hikes.

dimensions.name: Transparency
dimensions.notes: Borrower should see exactly how their rate is calculated.

source: RBI External Benchmark Lending Rate framework analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Non-compliance with EBLR mandate is reportable to RBI. Variable spreads violate the framework.
recommended_clause_language: Interest rate: RBI Repo Rate + [X]% fixed spread. Rate resets quarterly based on prevailing repo rate. Spread remains fixed for loan tenure.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'spread may be revised', 'internal benchmark rate', 'bank's prevailing rate', or missing rate reset frequency.

standard_description: Banking disputes in India have a well-established resolution hierarchy: (1) Bank's internal Grievance Redressal (15-30 days), (2) Banking Ombudsman (escalation if bank doesn't resolve within 30 days), (3) RBI's Complaint Management System (CMS), (4) Consumer Forum / NCLT for larger claims. The RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme 2021 provides a single-window mechanism for all bank complaints.
typical_range: Internal redressal (30 days) → Ombudsman → Consumer Forum

dimensions.name: Internal Grievance
dimensions.notes: Banks must acknowledge complaints within 3 working days.

dimensions.name: Ombudsman Access
dimensions.standard_value: Automatic escalation if unresolved in 30 days
dimensions.notes: Banking Ombudsman access is a regulatory right.

dimensions.name: Online Filing
dimensions.notes: cms.rbi.org.in allows online complaint filing.

dimensions.name: Compensation
dimensions.notes: Ombudsman can award up to ₹20 lakh; Consumer Forum is unlimited.

risk_if_exceeded: Banks that fail to resolve complaints within 30 days can be reported to the Banking Ombudsman under the RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme 2021.
recommended_clause_language: Disputes first raised with Bank's Grievance Officer. If unresolved within 30 days, complaint may be filed with Banking Ombudsman.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'mandatory arbitration', 'exclusive jurisdiction of courts in [bank's HQ city]', or 'waiver of Ombudsman rights' (all unenforceable).

standard_description: IRDAI mandates that health insurance claims must be settled within 30 days of receiving all required documents. Motor insurance claims should be settled within 30 days. If the insurer delays beyond this, they must pay interest on the claim amount. Cashless claims at network hospitals should be authorized within 2 hours for planned admissions and 4 hours for emergency admissions.
typical_range: 30 days from document submission; cashless authorization within 2-4 hours

dimensions.name: Health Claim Timeline
dimensions.notes: IRDAI regulation — delay requires interest payment.

dimensions.name: Cashless Authorization
dimensions.notes: IRDAI Health Insurance Regulations 2020 mandate this.

dimensions.name: Claim Rejection Reason
dimensions.standard_value: Written rejection with specific clause reference
dimensions.notes: Insurer must provide clear, written reasons for any rejection.

dimensions.name: Partial Settlement
dimensions.standard_value: Admitted amount paid immediately, balance disputed
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Full claim paid, disputes resolved separately
dimensions.notes: Undisputed portions should be paid without waiting.

source: IRDAI (Health Insurance) Regulations 2020 + claims data analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Claim settlement delays beyond 30 days entitle the policyholder to interest. Grievance escalation to IRDAI IGMS portal (igms.irda.gov.in).
recommended_clause_language: Claims settled within 30 days of complete documentation. Cashless authorization within 2 hours for planned admissions.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'claims will be processed as expeditiously as possible' (no timeline), excessive documentation requirements, or no cashless facility.

standard_description: Health insurance exclusions in India are regulated by IRDAI. Standard exclusions include: cosmetic surgery, self-inflicted injury, war/nuclear events, and pre-existing conditions (with mandated 4-year waiting period cap). IRDAI has standardized exclusion lists to prevent arbitrary denials. Day-1 coverage is mandatory for accidents. Specific disease waiting periods are capped at 2-4 years.
typical_range: Standardized IRDAI exclusions; PED waiting 1-4 years; disease waiting 2-4 years

dimensions.name: Pre-Existing Disease Waiting
dimensions.notes: IRDAI caps PED waiting at 4 years — cannot exceed this.

dimensions.name: Specific Disease Waiting
dimensions.notes: Common conditions like diabetes, hypertension typically 2-4 years.

dimensions.name: Day-1 Coverage
dimensions.notes: Accidents must be covered from policy start date.

dimensions.name: Sub-Limits
dimensions.standard_value: No room rent sub-limit or clearly disclosed
dimensions.strict_threshold: Hidden sub-limits on room rent, surgery, etc.
dimensions.notes: Sub-limits can drastically reduce effective coverage.

source: IRDAI standardized exclusion guidelines + policy analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Exclusions beyond IRDAI's standardized list or PED waiting periods exceeding 4 years violate IRDAI regulations.
recommended_clause_language: Exclusions as per IRDAI standard list. PED waiting period: 4 years. Accidents covered from day 1. All sub-limits clearly disclosed.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'permanent exclusion of pre-existing conditions', hidden room-rent sub-limits, or exclusions not on IRDAI's standard list.

standard_description: IRDAI mandates lifelong renewability for health insurance policies — the insurer cannot refuse renewal except for fraud or non-payment. Premium increases at renewal must be applied uniformly across the age band and cannot target individual policyholders. The insurer must provide renewal notice 30 days before expiry. Grace period for renewal is typically 30 days after expiry.
typical_range: Lifelong renewal guaranteed; 30-day advance notice; 30-day grace period

dimensions.name: Lifelong Renewability
dimensions.notes: Health insurance must be lifelong renewable — insurer cannot refuse.

dimensions.name: Premium Increase
dimensions.notes: Increases must apply uniformly, not target individuals with claims.

dimensions.name: Renewal Notice
dimensions.notes: Insurer must remind you before the policy lapses.

dimensions.name: Grace Period
dimensions.notes: Grace period allows renewal without losing continuity benefits.

risk_if_exceeded: Refusing to renew a health policy (except for fraud/non-payment) violates IRDAI mandate. Report to IGMS.
recommended_clause_language: Policy is lifelong renewable. Renewal notice provided 30 days before expiry. 30-day grace period for late renewal.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'renewal at insurer's discretion', individual-targeted premium increases, or no grace period clause.

standard_description: Health insurance premiums in India increase primarily due to age (age-band pricing) and medical inflation. Standard practice is 5-15% increases at age-band transitions (typically every 5 years). Insurers can also revise premiums due to claims experience, but must apply changes uniformly across the pool and file revised rates with IRDAI. Premium increases should be transparent and communicated in advance.
typical_range: 5-15% at age-band transitions; additional medical inflation adjustment

dimensions.name: Age-Band Increase
dimensions.notes: Age 45-55 typically sees the steepest increases.

dimensions.name: Claims-Based Revision
dimensions.strict_threshold: Individual premium hike for high claimers
dimensions.notes: IRDAI prohibits targeting individuals with premium hikes.

dimensions.name: No-Claim Bonus
dimensions.notes: NCB rewards healthy policyholders and reduces premium burden.

dimensions.name: Transparency
dimensions.standard_value: Premium revision communicated 30 days before renewal
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Premium schedule for all future age bands provided upfront
dimensions.notes: You should know what you'll pay 10 years from now.

source: IRDAI premium filing guidelines + industry analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Individual-targeted premium increases are prohibited by IRDAI. Unexplained premium hikes can be challenged via IGMS.
recommended_clause_language: Premium increases at age-band transitions only, applied uniformly. No-claim bonus of [X]% per claim-free year. Premium schedule for all age bands disclosed upfront.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'premium may be revised at insurer's discretion', no NCB benefit, or undisclosed future premium schedules.

standard_description: Insurance disputes in India are resolved through: (1) Insurer's internal Grievance Redressal (15 days), (2) IRDAI IGMS portal (igms.irda.gov.in), (3) Insurance Ombudsman (for claims up to ₹30 lakh), (4) Consumer Forum (unlimited amount). The Insurance Ombudsman is free, accessible, and decisions are binding on the insurer. Consumer Forum jurisdiction cannot be waived by contract.
typical_range: Internal (15 days) → IGMS → Ombudsman (₹30L) → Consumer Forum

dimensions.name: Internal Grievance
dimensions.notes: IRDAI mandates 15-day resolution timeline.

dimensions.name: Ombudsman Access
dimensions.standard_value: Free, for claims up to ₹30 lakh
dimensions.strict_threshold: Contract attempts to restrict Ombudsman access (void)
dimensions.notes: Insurance Ombudsman decisions are binding on the insurer.

dimensions.name: Consumer Forum
dimensions.strict_threshold: Arbitration clause attempting to oust Consumer Forum (void)
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Insurer settles before Consumer Forum hearing
dimensions.notes: Consumer Forum jurisdiction cannot be contractually waived.

dimensions.name: Claim Review Process
dimensions.strict_threshold: No appeal process for rejected claims
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Independent medical review for health claim disputes
dimensions.notes: Policyholders should have a clear appeal path.

source: Insurance Ombudsman Rules 2017 + IRDAI IGMS framework
risk_if_exceeded: Insurers that fail to resolve grievances within 15 days can be reported via IRDAI IGMS. Arbitration clauses cannot oust Consumer Forum.
recommended_clause_language: Complaints resolved within 15 days. Policyholder may approach Insurance Ombudsman or Consumer Forum at any time.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'mandatory arbitration', 'exclusive jurisdiction', or any clause attempting to waive Ombudsman/Consumer Forum access.

standard_description: Security deposits for residential rentals in India vary by state: Karnataka caps at 10 months' rent (commonly 10 months in Bengaluru), Maharashtra at 3 months, Delhi at 2-3 months, Tamil Nadu at 3-6 months. The Model Tenancy Act 2021 caps deposits at 2 months' rent. Deposits must be refunded within 1-3 months of vacating, after deducting only documented damages beyond normal wear and tear.
typical_range: 2-10 months rent (varies by state); refund within 1-3 months

dimensions.name: Deposit Amount
dimensions.notes: Bengaluru's 10-month convention is the highest in India; other cities are 2-3 months.

dimensions.name: Refund Timeline
dimensions.standard_value: Within 1 month of vacating (Model Tenancy Act)
dimensions.notes: Model Tenancy Act mandates refund within 1 month of move-out.

dimensions.name: Deduction Documentation
dimensions.strict_threshold: Deductions at landlord's discretion, no documentation
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Joint inspection + zero deductions for normal wear
dimensions.notes: Deductions must be for damages beyond normal wear and tear.

dimensions.name: Interest on Deposit
dimensions.notes: Maharashtra Rent Control Act requires interest on deposits.

source: Model Tenancy Act 2021 + state-wise Rent Control Act analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Non-refundable deposits and arbitrary deductions violate state Rent Acts. File complaint with Rent Authority or Consumer Forum.
recommended_clause_language: Security deposit of [X] months rent. Refundable within 30 days of vacating after deducting documented damages (with photographic evidence).
red_flag_language: Watch for 'non-refundable deposit', 'deductions at landlord's sole discretion', 'deposit forfeited for early termination', or no refund timeline.

standard_description: Standard rental agreements in India divide maintenance responsibilities: landlord handles structural repairs, plumbing/electrical infrastructure, and external painting; tenant handles day-to-day maintenance, minor repairs (typically up to ₹5,000-10,000), and internal cleanliness. The boundary should be clearly defined. Major appliances provided by the landlord (AC, geyser, washing machine) are typically the landlord's repair responsibility.
typical_range: Tenant: minor repairs up to ₹5,000-10,000; Landlord: structural and major repairs

dimensions.name: Minor Repairs
dimensions.standard_value: Tenant bears up to ₹5,000-10,000 per incident
dimensions.notes: Clear monetary threshold prevents disputes.

dimensions.name: Structural Repairs
dimensions.notes: Plumbing leaks, electrical wiring, roof — always landlord.

dimensions.name: Appliance Maintenance
dimensions.strict_threshold: Tenant maintains all appliances including landlord's
dimensions.lenient_threshold: Landlord provides AMC for all appliances
dimensions.notes: If the landlord provides an AC, they should maintain it.

dimensions.name: Painting/Restoration
dimensions.standard_value: Painting every 3-5 years is landlord's cost
dimensions.notes: Normal wall wear (nail holes, fading) is not tenant damage.

source: Model Tenancy Act 2021 + rental market practices analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Clauses making tenants responsible for structural repairs are unreasonable and challengeable under Rent Control Acts.
recommended_clause_language: Tenant handles minor repairs up to ₹[amount]. Structural, plumbing, and electrical repairs are Landlord's responsibility. Landlord-provided appliances maintained by Landlord.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'tenant shall maintain property in original condition', 'all repairs at tenant's cost', or 'mandatory painting before vacating'.

standard_description: Eviction in India is heavily regulated by state Rent Control Acts and the Model Tenancy Act 2021. Landlords can evict only for specified reasons: non-payment of rent, misuse of property, subletting without permission, or genuine personal need. The process requires legal notice, opportunity to cure defaults, and often a Rent Authority/Court order. Self-help eviction (changing locks, cutting utilities) is illegal.
typical_range: Legal notice → Opportunity to cure → Rent Authority order

dimensions.name: Grounds for Eviction
dimensions.standard_value: Only statutory grounds (non-payment, misuse, personal need)
dimensions.strict_threshold: Eviction 'at landlord's discretion' or 'for any reason'
dimensions.notes: Landlord cannot evict arbitrarily — must have statutory grounds.

dimensions.name: Notice Period
dimensions.notes: Model Tenancy Act requires adequate notice.

dimensions.name: Right to Cure
dimensions.standard_value: Tenant can cure default (pay arrears) to avoid eviction
dimensions.strict_threshold: No opportunity to cure before eviction
dimensions.notes: Most Rent Acts allow tenants to pay arrears and stay.

dimensions.name: Self-Help Eviction
dimensions.notes: Changing locks, cutting utilities, or physical eviction without court order is criminal.

source: Model Tenancy Act 2021 + state Rent Control Acts analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Self-help eviction (changing locks, cutting utilities) is criminal under IPC. Tenants can file FIR and seek relief from Rent Authority.
recommended_clause_language: Eviction only for statutory grounds with [2-3 months] written notice. Tenant has right to cure defaults. Eviction process through Rent Authority only.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'immediate eviction', 'landlord may reclaim without notice', 'eviction at landlord's discretion', or 'tenant waives eviction protections'.

standard_description: Annual rent escalation in Indian residential rentals typically ranges from 5-10%, with 5% being most common in agreement templates. Some states have Rent Control Acts that cap permissible increases. The Model Tenancy Act 2021 does not prescribe a specific cap but promotes 'fair and reasonable' increases. Commercial rent escalation is typically 10-15%. Escalation should only apply at annual or lease renewal intervals.

dimensions.name: Residential Annual Increase
dimensions.notes: 5% is the most common convention in Indian rental agreements.

dimensions.name: Commercial Annual Increase
dimensions.notes: Commercial rents are less regulated but should be predictable.

dimensions.name: Escalation Frequency
dimensions.notes: Escalation should be at fixed, predictable intervals.

dimensions.name: Rent Control Protection
dimensions.standard_value: Subject to state-specific caps where applicable
dimensions.strict_threshold: Agreement attempts to waive Rent Control protections
dimensions.notes: Tenants in Rent Control-covered properties have statutory caps.

source: Model Tenancy Act 2021 + state Rent Control Acts + market analysis
risk_if_exceeded: Rent increases exceeding state Rent Control caps (where applicable) are illegal. File complaint with Rent Authority.
recommended_clause_language: Rent shall increase by [5]% per annum, applicable on each anniversary of the lease commencement date.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'rent increase at landlord's discretion', 'quarterly escalation', escalation exceeding 10% for residential, or waiver of Rent Control protections.

standard_description: Lock-in periods in Indian residential rentals typically range from 3-12 months, during which neither party can terminate the agreement. After the lock-in, either party can terminate with 1-2 months' notice. Standard lock-in is 6 months for furnished apartments and 3-6 months for unfurnished. Penalty for breaking lock-in is typically 1-2 months' rent. Commercial leases have longer lock-ins (12-36 months).

dimensions.name: Residential Lock-In
dimensions.notes: 6 months is standard for furnished; 3 months for unfurnished.

dimensions.name: Commercial Lock-In
dimensions.notes: Longer lock-in justified for fit-out investment by tenant.

dimensions.name: Lock-In Penalty
dimensions.notes: Penalty should be proportional, not the entire remaining rent.

dimensions.name: Post Lock-In Notice
dimensions.notes: After lock-in, either party should be able to exit with notice.

source: Rental market analysis + Model Tenancy Act 2021
risk_if_exceeded: Lock-in periods exceeding 12 months for residential are unusual. Penalty equal to full remaining rent is disproportionate under Section 74, ICA.
recommended_clause_language: Lock-in period of [6] months. After lock-in, either party may terminate with [2] months written notice. Early termination during lock-in: [1] month's rent as penalty.
red_flag_language: Watch for 'entire remaining rent as penalty', lock-in exceeding 12 months for residential, or no post lock-in termination clause.
